items.myth: DBSCAN requires you to choose the number of clusters k like k-means.
items.reality: DBSCAN takes eps and min_samples, not k. Cluster count is an output determined by density connectivity — you tune hyperparameters, not k directly.
items.example: Same data with eps=0.3 may yield 4 clusters; eps=0.8 may merge to 1 cluster plus noise.

items.reality: Noise is a deliberate classification for sparse points not density-connected to any core — often the desired signal for outlier detection.
items.example: Fraud screening: 2% noise label may be the transactions sent for manual review.

items.myth: Any point within eps of a cluster centroid belongs to that cluster.
items.reality: Membership follows density reachability from core points, not distance to a centroid. Border points join via a core neighbor; isolated nearby points can still be noise.
items.example: Point near a cluster visually but with only one neighbor within eps → noise, not border.

items.myth: DBSCAN and k-means always disagree because they use different k.
items.reality: They optimize different geometry: k-means minimizes within-centroid variance with fixed k; DBSCAN chains dense eps-neighborhoods. On spherical equal-density blobs they can agree roughly after tuning.
items.example: Three well-separated Gaussian blobs on scaled 2D data — both methods may find three groups with careful eps or k.
